title: Learning dynamics in linear recurrent neural networks

reviews.summary: This paper develops an analytical framework for learning dynamics in linear recurrent neural networks (LRNNs) that explicitly incorporates temporal task structure. Under whitened inputs, constant singular/eigenvectors over time, and data–model alignment, the authors diagonalize training dynamics into per-mode “connectivity modes” (input a, recurrent b, output c) and derive a closed-form energy (loss) function E = (1/2τ) Σ_i (s_i − c b^{T−i} a)^2. From this, they obtain four central insights: (i) learning is ordered jointly by singular value magnitude and temporal position (later singular values are learned faster when |b|<1), (ii) task dynamics determine stability and extrapolation (e.g., “early-importance” tasks induce b>1 and instability; some tasks do not extrapolate across sequence lengths T), (iii) the loss contains an effective regularization term that favors small weights and induces a sharp phase transition between feedforward and recurrent computations (leading to low-rank solutions), and (iv) recurrence promotes feature learning, supported by a finite-width LRNN neural tangent kernel (NTK) derivation and kernel-distance experiments. The framework also predicts and explains solutions and extrapolation behavior in a sensory integration task without the alignment/whitening assumptions.
strengths: Provides a clean, closed-form per-mode energy (and associated ODEs) for LRNN learning that decouples across singular/eigen dimensions under stated assumptions, enabling transparent analytical insight into temporal learning.
Identifies and analyzes the joint role of singular value magnitude and temporal precedence in ordering of learning, a departure from the standard “largest SV first” rule in feedforward linear nets.

Establishes a principled link between task dynamics and stability/extrapolation (e.g., inverse-exponential vs exponential vs constant), including explicit zero-loss solutions and conditions that force b>1 and instability.

Shows that the loss decomposes into a data-driven term plus an effective “regularizer” term that biases towards small weights and mediates a first-order phase transition between feedforward and recurrent computation (analytically characterized for Dirac-delta dynamics).
Derives an NTK for finite-width LRNNs and uses it to argue that increasing recurrence (longer T) promotes feature learning beyond purely feedforward settings.
Simulations consistently match theoretical predictions across multiple task dynamics and parameter sweeps; the phase-transition behavior is clearly demonstrated.
Sensory-integration experiments, conducted without the main alignment/whitening assumptions, validate key theoretical predictions (solutions for sum/mean and extrapolation differences).
The paper is well-structured and intuitive: decomposing s_t as s_t = δ f(λ,t) neatly separates scaling from dynamics; figures are informative and align with the stated claims.
Assumptions are explicit and their plausibility/limitations are discussed; appendices provide substantial derivational detail and extensions (e.g., eigen-decomposition for rotations, T-output case).
Bridges learning dynamics and RNN dynamical systems by tying training outcomes directly to temporal task structure, stability, extrapolation, and representational rank.

Offers practical conceptual guidance (e.g., why early-importance tasks can be unstable; when extrapolation across sequence lengths will fail; when recurrent modes get pruned), relevant for both ML and neuroscience communities.
weaknesses: Core results rely on strong assumptions: whitened inputs across time, constant singular/eigenvectors over time, and early alignment with data directions; while motivated and sometimes empirically reasonable, these assumptions limit generality.

The “effective regularization” interpretation arises from an algebraic decomposition of the same data loss; while insightful, it is not an external regularizer and its role could be overstated without further qualification (e.g., dependency on |b| and T).
Some statements could be sharpened technically (e.g., divergence of the “regularizer” term as T→∞ depends on |b|≥1 and nonzero ac, not on “cba >> 0” per se).

Empirical validation is primarily on synthetic settings tailored to the assumptions; more systematic tests with temporally correlated inputs (beyond the brief “relaxed assumptions” demo) would strengthen claims of robustness.

NTK results focus on kernel-distance movement; additional metrics (e.g., prediction performance vs infinite-width NTK baselines, width-scaling laws) could better situate the “recurrence encourages feature learning” claim.

The precise conditions under which the zero-loss solutions exist could be summarized more carefully in the main text (e.g., b=1 case and stability boundary; constant dynamics as special case of inverse-exponential with λ=1).
The role and range of validity of the gradient-flow regime (small learning rate) could be discussed more explicitly in relation to discrete-time training and SGD noise.

The paper would benefit from a sharper contrast with closely related recent analyses of LRNN learning and feature vs kernel regimes (e.g., recurrent NTK formulations; continuous-time LRNN analyses showing emergent outliers and rich/lazy transitions). While several are cited, a more explicit side-by-side analytical comparison would aid positioning.
Additional discussion of Emami et al. (implicit bias in linear RNNs) and how the present “effective regularization” perspective relates would be valuable.
reviews.questions:
1. In Lemma 3.2, can you clarify the exact conditions for divergence of the “effective regularization” term as T→∞ (e.g., dependence on |b|≥1 and nonzero ac), and whether any conclusions change when |b|<1 but close to 1?
2. How robust are your main qualitative findings (learning order, stability/extrapolation, phase transitions) when inputs are temporally correlated and not whitened? Could you provide an experiment with correlated inputs (without pre-whitening) and report deviations?
3. The alignment assumption is central to mode-wise decoupling. Do you have quantitative evidence (e.g., angle to data singular vectors over training time) that alignment emerges early in LRNNs with small random weights, beyond the sensory-integration case?
4. How does your finite-width LRNN-NTK compare analytically to existing recurrent NTK formulations (e.g., Alemohammad et al., Yang’s general architecture results)? Are there closed-form expressions unique to your linear recurrent setting, and can you report width-scaling trends of kernel movement?
5. For the T-output (autoregressive) setting, which of your main conclusions (e.g., instability with early-importance, extrapolation failure for T-dependent solutions) still hold and which change materially? Can you summarize any notable differences observed empirically?
6. The “only inverse-exponential dynamics” have zero-loss solutions per mode. How does the theory extend to mixtures or piecewise dynamics (e.g., sums of exponentials)? Can multiple modes jointly achieve near-perfect fits that individual modes cannot?
7. Could architectural modifications (e.g., gating or normalization) mitigate the instability for early-importance tasks by effectively controlling |b|, and how would your framework predict such effects?
reviews.assessment:
This paper offers a clear and insightful analytical treatment of LRNN learning that explicitly incorporates temporal task structure, yielding nontrivial and practically relevant conclusions about learning order, stability, extrapolation, and implicit low-rank bias. The per-mode energy perspective is elegant and, together with the s_t = δ f(λ,t) decomposition, makes several otherwise opaque phenomena transparent. The identification of phase transitions between feedforward and recurrent computation, and the NTK-based evidence that increasing recurrence promotes feature learning, are both timely and valuable.

The principal caveat is generality: the main theorems hinge on strong (though motivated) assumptions—whitened inputs, constant singular/eigenvectors, and alignment—and results are developed under gradient-flow. The empirical section, while supportive, remains mostly within synthetic regimes; strengthening validation under departures from these assumptions (e.g., temporally correlated inputs, misalignment) and deepening connections to recurrent NTK and recent LRNN learning theories would further solidify the contribution.

Overall, I find the work to be technically sound within scope, well-presented, and of clear interest to the ICML community at the intersection of learning dynamics, sequence modeling, and computational neuroscience. I recommend acceptance, contingent on clarifying the noted technical points and sharpening comparisons to closely related recent work.
